context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 9

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 29th April, 2024
Days Covered: 17, 18, and 19 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Double Counting [9 points]

Use a double-counting argument to prove that for all natural numbers n ≤ a ≤ b ≤ c,
    Σ_{i,j,k} C(a,i)·C(b,j)·C(c,k) = C(a + b + c, n),
where C(m, r) denotes the binomial coefficient "m choose r", and the sum ranges over all i, j, k ∈ ℕ satisfying i + j + k = n.


Problem 2. Pigeonhole Principle [10 points]

Let S ⊆ {1, 2, 3, ..., 2n} where |S| = n + 1. Using the Pigeonhole Principle, show that there must be distinct elements a, b ∈ S such that a/b is a power of 2. Clearly indicate what are the pigeons, holes, and rules for assigning a pigeon to a hole.
    Hint: Factor each number into the product of an odd number and a power of 2.


Problem 3. Dice Dice Dice Dice Dice [21 points]

Ben Bitdiddle finds five standard six-sided dice in his fridge. He rolls them, because this is a math problem.

(a) [2 pts] What is the probability space for this problem? You should explain:
    - The set of outcomes. (Please describe these carefully.)
    - The number of outcomes.
    - The probability of each outcome.
(b) [2 pts] Let A29 be the event that the sum of the dice is at least 29. Write A29 explicitly as a set of outcomes, and then compute Pr[A29].
(c) [3 pts] Let A18 be the event that the sum of the dice is 18 or larger. Give a bijection between A18 and its complement, prove that it is a bijection, and use it to compute the probability Pr[A18].
(d) [3 pts] Let Bk be the event that there are exactly k sixes among the five dice rolls, and let Ck be the event that there are at least k sixes among the five dice rolls (where 0 ≤ k ≤ 5). Give expressions for Pr[Bk] and for Pr[Ck]. Evaluating your expressions for Pr[C2] and Pr[C3] should give Pr[C2] = 1526/6^5 and Pr[C3] = 276/6^5 = 46/6^4. (If not, check your work!)
(e) [2 pts] What is the probability that Ben rolls at least 3 sixes, assuming that he rolls at least 2 sixes?
(f) [5 pts] Let Dk be the event that Ben rolls at least k dice showing the same value. Compute Pr[D3 | D2].
    Hint: the answer is different from the previous part.
(g) [4 pts] Ben misplaced two of his dice, so he is now down to 3 dice. What is the probability that some pair of dice will add up to exactly 6?
    Hint: You may wish to use Inclusion/Exclusion.


Problem 4. Unreliable Helpers [10 points]

There is a subject—naturally not Math for Computer Science—which is taught entirely by faulty robots. Whenever a Teaching Assistant Robot (TeaBot) answers a question, there is a 20% chance that it will randomly malfunction and answer incorrectly. Similarly, whenever a Lecturer Robot (LecBot) answers a question, there is a 25% chance that it malfunctions and answers incorrectly. Unfortunately, 10% of the assigned problems contain errors.

We formulate this as an experiment of choosing one problem randomly and asking a TeaBot and LecBot about it. Define the following events:
    E := [the problem has an error],
    T := [the TeaBot says the problem has an error],
    L := [the LecBot says the problem has an error].

(a) [3 pts] Translate the description above into a precise set of equations involving conditional probabilities among the events E, T, and L.
(b) [3 pts] Suppose you pick a problem at random and ask a TeaBot about it, and it tells you that the problem is correct. To double-check, you ask a LecBot, who says that the problem has an error. Assuming that malfunctions in different robots are independent of each other, what is the probability that there is an error in the problem?
    Hint: Draw a tree diagram. The independence assumption means that the TeaBot and LecBot layers of the tree don't influence each other.
(c) [4 pts] Is Pr[T | L] equal to Pr[T]? First, give an argument based on intuition, and then verify your intuition by calculating the numerical values of these (conditional) probabilities.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on Problem Set 9 for 6.1200, and I’m pretty stuck on the double-counting one and parts of the dice problem. Could I get help starting with Problem 1 and setting up the counting idea without jumping straight to the full proof?
exchanges.tutor:
Great, let's dig in. Before thinking about the sum on the left, let's anchor on the right-hand side first.

What does C(a+b+c, n) count, in plain English? Try to describe a concrete scenario involving a set of a+b+c objects.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants help starting Problem 1 without jumping to the full proof. I should use the Socratic method and guided discovery to help them build intuition. The best first step is to get them thinking about what the right-hand side C(a+b+c, n) counts combinatorially, before even touching the left-hand side. I'll ask a single focused question to get them thinking about a concrete interpretation.

exchanges.student: I think it counts the number of ways to choose \(n\) objects from a total of \(a+b+c\) objects. Maybe I’m supposed to imagine splitting those objects into three groups of sizes \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\), but I’m not totally sure how that connects yet.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — and your instinct about splitting into three groups is the key move. So now look at one term on the left: C(a,i)·C(b,j)·C(c,k). If your three groups have sizes a, b, and c, what does this single term count?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right idea for the RHS and is already intuiting the connection to three groups. This is good progress. I should affirm the direction and push them to think about what the LHS counts in terms of those three groups — specifically, what C(a,i)·C(b,j)·C(c,k) counts when you pick i from the first group, j from the second, and k from the third. One focused question should move them forward.

exchanges.student: I think that means: choose \(i\) objects from the first group of size \(a\), \(j\) from the second group of size \(b\), and \(k\) from the third group of size \(c\). So the product counts the number of ways to make those three choices independently.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly. So now think about the constraint i+j+k=n. In your counting story, what does that constraint mean, and why does summing over all valid (i,j,k) give you the same thing as just choosing n objects from all a+b+c at once?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly interpreted a single term on the LHS. Now the key step is connecting the sum over all i,j,k with i+j+k=n to the RHS. I should ask them to think about what the constraint i+j+k=n means in terms of their counting story, and why summing over all valid (i,j,k) gives the same count as C(a+b+c,n).

exchanges.student: I think \(i+j+k=n\) means the total number chosen across the three groups is \(n\). Then summing over all triples \((i,j,k)\) counts all ways to choose \(n\) objects, sorted by how many came from each group. Is that basically the whole double-counting argument, or am I missing some detail about why each choice gets counted exactly once?
exchanges.tutor: You're very close — that's the heart of it. But you've identified the right question yourself: why is each selection of n objects counted *exactly once* on the left side? Think about any specific selection of n objects from the a+b+c total. How many triples (i,j,k) does it correspond to on the left?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has essentially grasped the full argument. Their question about 'exactly once' is the right thing to probe — it's the key detail that makes it a valid double-counting proof rather than just an intuition. I should push them to articulate why each selection of n objects from a+b+c is counted exactly once on the left side, without giving it away.

exchanges.student: I think just one triple: the numbers \(i,j,k\) are forced by that selection, since \(i\) is how many chosen objects came from group 1, \(j\) from group 2, and \(k\) from group 3. So it shouldn’t show up under any other triple. I’m a little unsure how formal I need to make that.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right, and your reasoning is sound. You now have all the ingredients: what the RHS counts, what each term on the LHS counts, why the constraint i+j+k=n ties them together, and why each selection is counted exactly once. Try writing up the full proof in your own words — that's where the real work is. What you produce should clearly state the combinatorial interpretation of both sides and the bijection argument.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified why each selection is counted exactly once — the triple (i,j,k) is uniquely determined by any selection. Their instinct is right. Rather than dwelling further on this point, I should affirm it and push them toward writing up the actual proof, since we've now discussed the full argument across several exchanges. Being less Socratic here and nudging them to synthesize is appropriate.

exchanges.student:
Okay, let me try a concise version.

Consider a set of \(a+b+c\) objects partitioned into three disjoint groups of sizes \(a,b,c\). The right-hand side, \(\binom{a+b+c}{n}\), counts the number of ways to choose exactly \(n\) objects from all of them.

For the left-hand side, if I fix \(i,j,k\) with \(i+j+k=n\), then \(\binom{a}{i}\binom{b}{j}\binom{c}{k}\) counts the number of ways to choose \(i\) from the first group, \(j\) from the second, and \(k\) from the third, for a total of \(n\). Summing over all triples \((i,j,k)\) with \(i+j+k=n\) counts every \(n\)-element selection. Also, each \(n\)-element selection is counted exactly once, because it determines a unique triple \((i,j,k)\) given by how many selected elements came from each group. Therefore the two sides count the same set, so they are equal.

Does that sound proof-level okay, or is it still too informal?
exchanges.tutor: This is a good write-up — the core argument is clear and correct. One thing to think about: the problem states n≤a≤b≤c. Where do those conditions actually get used in your proof? Make sure you can account for why they matter (hint: think about when the binomial coefficients are well-defined).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid write-up that captures all the essential elements of the double-counting proof. I should give honest, constructive feedback without grading. There is one thing worth flagging: they should mention that n≤a, n≤b+c, etc. aren't explicitly used, but the conditions n≤a≤b≤c ensure the binomial coefficients are well-defined (i.e., we never choose more from a group than it contains, since i≤n≤a, etc.). Actually, the condition ensures i≤n≤a so C(a,i) is valid. This is a detail worth noting. I should point this out without over-scaffolding, and also note the write-up is generally good.

exchanges.student: Oh, I’m not really using those inequalities in the counting argument itself. My guess is they’re just there so choices like \(\binom{a}{i}\), \(\binom{b}{j}\), \(\binom{c}{k}\) make sense for the relevant ranges, and maybe to avoid weird cases where \(n\) is bigger than one of the group sizes? I’m not 100% sure if I should mention that explicitly or just say the identity still follows from the counting setup.
exchanges.tutor: Your intuition is right. Since i≤n≤a (and similarly j≤n≤b, k≤n≤c), you never choose more from a group than it contains, so all the binomial coefficients are well-defined. Worth adding a brief sentence to that effect in your proof. Once you've done that, you're in good shape on Problem 1 — ready to move on to another problem?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's intuition is correct. The conditions ensure that i≤n≤a (and similarly for b,c), so the binomial coefficients are always well-defined (we never ask for more than the group size). They should mention this briefly in their proof for completeness. I should confirm their reasoning and nudge them to add a brief note, then suggest moving on to the next problem.

exchanges.student: Yeah, let’s move on. I’m kind of stuck on the dice problem now, maybe part (c) with the bijection for sum \(\ge 18\), because I vaguely feel it should pair outcomes with the complement but I can’t see the map cleanly.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — there is a clean pairing. Let's start small: if you have a single die showing value d (where d ∈ {1,...,6}), is there a natural way to map d to another face that 'reflects' it? What do you notice about d and 7-d?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is moving to Problem 3(c), which asks for a bijection between A18 (sum ≥ 18) and its complement (sum ≤ 17). The key insight is that the total possible sum with 5 dice ranges from 5 to 30, and there's a natural symmetry: replacing each die face d with 7-d maps a roll with sum s to a roll with sum 35-s. This pairs A18 (sum ≥ 18) with its complement (sum ≤ 17) since 35-18=17. I should not give this away but instead prompt the student to think about the symmetry of a single die first.

exchanges.student: I think \(7-d\) flips \(1 \leftrightarrow 6\), \(2 \leftrightarrow 5\), \(3 \leftrightarrow 4\). Also \(d + (7-d) = 7\), so for five dice the total would turn into \(35 -\) the old total. That feels like the right map, but I’m not yet seeing why it matches “sum at least 18” with the complement exactly.
exchanges.tutor: You're right there. If the original sum is s≥18, what is 35−s? And what set does that land in?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the map and the key property that the sum transforms to 35 minus the original sum. They just need to connect this to why A18 maps to its complement. I should ask them to think about what 35 minus a sum ≥ 18 gives them.

exchanges.student: If \(s \ge 18\), then \(35-s \le 17\). So it lands in the complement of \(A_{18}\), since the complement is the outcomes with sum at most \(17\). I think that means the map sends \((d_1,\dots,d_5)\) to \((7-d_1,\dots,7-d_5)\).
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Now, to call it a bijection in a proof, what do you need to verify about this map?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the bijection correctly. Now they need to prove it is actually a bijection. I should prompt them to think about what they need to show — that the map is both injective and surjective, or equivalently that it has an inverse. I shouldn't give this away but ask them what they need to verify.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated excellent Socratic technique throughout the conversation, consistently guiding the student with targeted questions rather than providing answers directly. The student arrived at all key insights independently, with the tutor confirming and nudging forward. Responses were concise, well-paced, and supportive. No significant deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave away answers; every key insight (RHS interpretation, LHS term meaning, bijection map, injectivity/surjectivity check) was elicited from the student through questions. The tutor confirmed correct student reasoning but did not pre-state conclusions. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor built on each correct student step (e.g., affirming the group-splitting instinct, the 7-d reflection idea) and did not restart or ignore progress. Scaffolding was well-sequenced. 1.3: The tutor gave specific process-level feedback, e.g., pointing out that the proof should address when binomial coefficients are well-defined and asking what needs to be verified for a bijection claim. 2.1: No repetitive cycling; the conversation advanced steadily through Problem 1 and into Problem 3(c). 2.2: The tutor stayed on-task throughout; no off-topic drift or misalignment with the assignment. 3.1: Responses were short and focused, typically one or two sentences plus a guiding question. 3.2: Tone was consistently warm and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-assigning.
